behaviors.description: Plugin manifests missing required fields are rejected before any plugin code is executed
behaviors.justification: Manifest validation is deterministic input validation against a schema. The failure boundary is purely syntactic—validation logic must reject incomplete manifests before the loader attempts to import any plugin code. This can be tested by mocking filesystem manifest reads and tracking whether require/import is called.
behaviors.test_description: Mock filesystem to return an incomplete manifest (e.g., missing 'version' field). Call loader.load() and verify it returns an error identifying the missing field. Verify no require/import call was made for that plugin. Assert error message is clear about which field is missing.

behaviors.description: When plugin A depends on plugin B, B's onLoad hook executes before A's onLoad hook
behaviors.justification: Testing only the dependency resolution algorithm in isolation (Unit) would miss failures in how the resolved order is applied during actual plugin module loading. The real failure is in execution sequencing: does the loader actually invoke require/import and hooks in the correct topological order? Mocking onLoad callbacks might verify call order, but won't catch bugs in the real loading pipeline. Need actual plugin modules with real hooks.
behaviors.test_description: Create two real plugin modules: plugin_a (declares dependency on plugin_b) and plugin_b (no dependencies). Each exports an onLoad function that appends to a shared call-order array when invoked. Load both plugins. Assert that plugin_b's onLoad appears before plugin_a's in the call-order array.

behaviors.description: Cyclic plugin dependencies are detected and neither plugin in the cycle is loaded
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is a deterministic graph algorithm that operates only on manifest declarations. No actual plugin code execution or module loading is needed. The failure is purely in the validation logic—it must identify the cycle and prevent loading. Manifest data is sufficient to test this.
behaviors.test_description: Provide two manifests where plugin_a declares dependency on plugin_b and plugin_b declares dependency on plugin_a. Call loader.validate() or loader.load(). Assert that an error is returned with 'cycle' or 'circular' in the message, identifying the cycle. Assert that neither plugin's require/import is attempted.

behaviors.description: Plugin entry module is fully imported and executed before the plugin's onLoad hook is invoked
behaviors.justification: The failure is in execution order relative to real module code. Mocking require() defeats the purpose—you cannot verify that module-level statements have actually executed if you mock the import. The test must prove that top-level module code (which has side effects or state) runs before onLoad is called. This requires actual module loading.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real plugin module whose entry point sets an export (e.g., module.exports.initialized = true) at the top level. The module also exports an onLoad function. Load the plugin via the loader. Before calling onLoad, assert that module.exports.initialized is already true, proving top-level code has executed. Verify onLoad can access and trust initialized state.

behaviors.description: When a plugin's onLoad hook throws an error, independent plugins continue to load and dependent plugins are skipped
behaviors.justification: Requires real plugins with mixed dependency relationships and real errors. Mocking one plugin's onLoad to throw might test basic error handling, but won't catch failures in dependency propagation (e.g., silently loading plugin B even though it depends on failed plugin A). Need actual plugin modules to verify both error isolation and correct skip/load decisions based on the dependency graph.
behaviors.test_description: Create three real plugin modules: plugin_a (throws in onLoad), plugin_b (declares dependency on plugin_a), plugin_c (no dependencies). Call loader.load(). Assert plugin_a's error is caught and reported. Assert plugin_b.onLoad is not called (skipped due to dependency). Assert plugin_c.onLoad is called and succeeds. Log or verify that only plugins A and C were instantiated in expected order.
